01-01-07.description: The high-flow air filter exhibits complete saturation with oil mist, salt particles, and combustion debris, resulting in a 40-60% reduction in airflow capacity. Visual inspection reveals a dark brown/black discoloration across the filter media with possible clogging of micro-pores. Engine performance degrades with rough idling at 800-900 RPM (normal: 1200 RPM) and increased exhaust smoke opacity. The restriction causes the turbocharger to work harder, producing a higher-pitched whining sound during acceleration phases.
detailed_steps: Execute complete engine lockout/tagout procedure and allow engine to cool below 60°C before beginning work
Disconnect negative battery terminal and remove engine cover panels to access air intake housing
Use digital manometer to measure differential pressure across filter (should be <2.5 kPa when clean)
Remove air intake housing bolts using filter housing wrench set, noting bolt torque specifications of 85 Nm
Extract saturated filter element ENG-FL-01 and inspect housing for debris accumulation or damage
Install new High-Flow Air Filter ensuring proper seating against housing gasket with no air gaps
Reassemble housing with bolts torqued to specification (85 Nm) in cross-pattern sequence
Perform post-installation pressure test and verify engine idle returns to 1200 RPM ± 50 RPM
01-01-07.quick_fix: LOCKOUT -> REMOVE HOUSING -> EXTRACT FILTER -> INSTALL NEW ENG-FL-01 -> TORQUE BOLTS -> TEST PRESSURE

01-02-07.description: Excessive carbon deposits accumulate on spark plug electrodes, creating a thick black coating that reduces spark intensity and causes misfiring. The buildup appears as a sooty, dry carbon layer approximately 1-2mm thick on the electrode surfaces. Engine exhibits irregular firing patterns with audible misfiring every 3-5 seconds during idle, accompanied by unburned fuel odor in exhaust gases. Cylinder head temperature increases 15-20°C above normal operating range due to incomplete combustion cycles.
detailed_steps: Implement lockout/tagout procedure and allow engine to cool to ambient temperature (minimum 2-hour cooldown)
Remove ignition coil assemblies and disconnect spark plug wire harnesses from each cylinder
Use compressed air to clear debris from spark plug wells before removal to prevent contamination
Remove old spark plugs using appropriate spark plug socket, noting electrode gap measurements with feeler gauge
Apply thin layer of anti-seize compound to new ENG-SP-02 iridium plug threads (avoid electrode contact)
Install new spark plugs hand-tight plus 1/2 turn, then torque to 30 Nm using calibrated torque wrench
Reconnect ignition coils and wire harnesses ensuring proper cylinder firing order sequence
Perform engine start test and verify smooth idle at 1200 RPM with no misfiring detected
01-02-07.quick_fix: LOCKOUT -> REMOVE COILS -> EXTRACT OLD PLUGS -> INSTALL ENG-SP-02 -> TORQUE 30NM -> RECONNECT -> TEST

01-03-05.description: Electronic fuel injector timing deviates from optimal injection point by 5-8 degrees, causing incomplete fuel atomization and combustion knock. The malfunction produces a distinct metallic knocking sound during power strokes, particularly under load conditions above 75% engine capacity. Fuel consumption increases by 15-25% while power output decreases proportionally. Exhaust gas temperature rises 40-60°C above normal, and unburned hydrocarbon emissions increase significantly. The ECU may log multiple timing fault codes as the injector fails to respond within the required 2-millisecond window.
detailed_steps: Execute complete fuel system lockout/tagout and depressurize fuel rails to 0 PSI using pressure relief valve
Disconnect fuel rail and electrical connections to affected injector, marking wire positions for reassembly
Use fuel line disconnect tools to remove high-pressure fuel lines without damaging quick-connect fittings
Remove defective injector valve and inspect fuel rail seat for damage or debris accumulation
Install new ENG-IN-05 Electronic Injector Valve with new O-rings torqued to 45 Nm specification
Reconnect fuel lines and electrical harnesses, ensuring proper polarity and connection integrity
Pressurize fuel system to operating pressure (350 PSI) and check for leaks using pressure tester
Use electronic timing light to verify injection timing matches ECU specifications within ±2 degrees
01-03-05.quick_fix: LOCKOUT -> DEPRESSURIZE FUEL -> DISCONNECT LINES -> REPLACE ENG-IN-05 -> RECONNECT -> PRESSURE TEST -> TIMING CHECK

01-01-05.description: The sealed roller bearing in the coolant pump exhibits advanced wear patterns with metal-on-metal contact producing a grinding noise audible from 10-15 meters away. Bearing clearance exceeds specification by 0.3-0.5mm, causing pump shaft wobble and reduced coolant flow rate from 450 L/min to approximately 280 L/min. Metallic debris appears in coolant system, and pump housing temperature increases 25-35°C above normal. Vibration amplitude measures 8-12mm/s (normal: <2mm/s) indicating imminent bearing failure and potential pump seizure.
detailed_steps: Implement complete cooling system lockout/tagout and drain coolant to level below pump housing
Disconnect coolant lines and remove pump assembly from engine block using appropriate lifting equipment
Disassemble pump housing and use bearing puller set to extract worn BRG-CP-11 bearing from shaft
Inspect pump shaft for scoring or damage, measuring runout with dial indicator (must be <0.05mm)
Use hydraulic press to install new Sealed Roller Bearing ensuring proper seating depth of 15.2mm
Reassemble pump housing with new gaskets and torque bolts to 150 Nm in specified sequence
Reinstall pump assembly and refill cooling system, bleeding air pockets through designated bleed valves
Perform operational test verifying coolant flow rate returns to 450 L/min ± 25 L/min specification
01-01-05.quick_fix: LOCKOUT -> DRAIN COOLANT -> REMOVE PUMP -> EXTRACT BEARING -> PRESS NEW BRG-CP-11 -> REASSEMBLE -> TEST FLOW

01-02-99.description: Complete structural failure of the piston rod assembly with visible crack propagation through the connecting rod forging, typically occurring at the small end bushing interface. The fracture produces catastrophic engine noise including violent knocking, metal-on-metal impacts, and potential secondary damage to cylinder walls and crankshaft. Engine immediately loses compression in the affected cylinder, with compression pressure dropping from 180 PSI to <20 PSI. Metallic debris contaminates the oil system, and severe vibration (>25mm/s) occurs throughout the engine block. Immediate shutdown is mandatory to prevent complete engine destruction.
detailed_steps: Execute emergency engine shutdown and complete lockout/tagout, allowing 4-hour minimum cooldown period
Remove cylinder head assembly and oil pan to access damaged piston/rod assembly from above and below
Use engine hoist to carefully extract fractured ENG-PR-99 components, inspecting for secondary damage to block
Measure cylinder bore dimensions with bore gauge to verify roundness within 0.025mm tolerance
Install new Full Piston/Rod Assembly using ring compressor tool and verify bearing clearances with Plastigage
Torque connecting rod bolts to 320 Nm specification using precision torque wrench in two-stage sequence
Reassemble engine components with new gaskets and perform complete oil system flush to remove debris
Conduct comprehensive engine test including compression test (target: 180 PSI ± 10 PSI) and vibration analysis
01-02-99.quick_fix: EMERGENCY SHUTDOWN -> LOCKOUT -> REMOVE HEAD/PAN -> EXTRACT DAMAGED PARTS -> INSTALL ENG-PR-99 -> TORQUE 320NM -> FLUSH OIL -> COMPRESSION TEST
